name: Acceptable Uses in Automated Scoring Search Pass

search_queries.result_count: screened first result page for targeted query
screened first result page for targeted query
screened first result page for targeted query
screened first result page for targeted query

search_queries.query: "Leveraging Knowledge Profiles" "Realistic Student Response Generation" automated grading model training
search_queries.result_count: identified abstract-level/metadata candidate but not full text

search_queries.query: "Best of both worlds" "synthetic" "automated scoring" "situational judgment test"
"A Framework for Live Scoring Constructed Response Items" synthetic responses pre-pilot validation
"Is Lunch Free Yet" "LLM-Generated Training Data" content scoring

inclusion_criteria: The source must involve synthetic, simulated, generated, corrupted, or analogous student/learner/examinee responses, essays, writing responses, short answers, constructed responses, or open-response assessment responses.

Direct support must connect the synthetic responses to automated scoring research, scorer development, training augmentation, pre-pilot validation, calibration, benchmarking, label/annotation workflow, challenge/stress testing, fairness probing, or deployment-readiness boundaries.
Direct findings require full text, full-text HTML, PDF, or previously verified full-text source records with source locators beyond an abstract-only page.
The source must distinguish synthetic-response evidence from real-response or independently human-scored validation evidence.
exclusion_criteria: Exclude automated scoring papers that score only authentic student responses and do not use synthetic responses.
Exclude synthetic-response generation work that does not connect generated responses to scorer research, scorer development, scoring labels, scorer evaluation, or scoring-system stress tests.
Exclude abstract-only or paywalled candidates from direct synthesis support until full text is obtained.
Exclude generic LLM-as-judge or human-AI scoring agreement work unless synthetic student responses are part of the scored corpus.
dedupe_method: Deduplicated by title, DOI, arXiv identifier, ACL Anthology identifier, canonical source page, and existing public graph source_id.

screening_counts.records_identified: at least 35 records across targeted web, primary-source, and existing-graph queries

screening_decisions.title: A Framework for Live Scoring Constructed Response Items with Commercial LLMs
screening_decisions.reason: Direct full-text evidence on synthetic responses as pre-pilot scorer checks and challenge sets, with real student responses required for pilot validation.

screening_decisions.title: DREsS: Dataset for Rubric-based Essay Scoring on EFL Writing
screening_decisions.reason: Direct full-text evidence on a large synthetic training subset for AES while real classroom essays remain the validation/test evidence.

screening_decisions.title: Is Lunch Free Yet? Overcoming the Cold-Start Problem in Supervised Content Scoring using Zero-Shot LLM-Generated Training Data
screening_decisions.reason: Direct full-text evidence that generated target labels need audit or relabeling before they support content-scoring model development.

screening_decisions.title: End-to-End Automated Item Generation and Scoring for Adaptive English Writing Assessment with Large Language Models
screening_decisions.reason: Direct full-text evidence on LLM annotator selection against expert human ratings before large-scale synthetic response annotation and scoring-model integration.

screening_decisions.title: Calibrating Generative AI to Produce Realistic Essays for Data Augmentation
screening_decisions.reason: Direct full-text evidence on expert-scored simulated essays as calibration and augmentation-suitability evidence, not synthetic-only scorer validation.

screening_decisions.title: Best of both worlds: combining LLMs and traditional ML for automated scoring of an open-response situational judgment test
screening_decisions.reason: Full-text adjacent evidence on synthetic demographic analogs for fairness stress testing in open-response automated scoring; included with SJT admissions-response boundary.

screening_decisions.title: Leveraging Knowledge Profiles and Generative AI for Realistic Student Response Generation
screening_decisions.reason: Highly relevant to synthetic response augmentation for automated grading, but this pass only verified repository/abstract-level access; not used as direct support.

screening_decisions.title: Using recursively generated synthetic responses to validate item-level variational encoder latent representations
screening_decisions.reason: AIME program listing suggests relevance to synthetic responses and validation, but no full paper was verified in this targeted pass.

screening_decisions.title: A Multilingual Dataset of Student Answers, Human Grading, and Multi-LLM Evaluations for Automated Assessment Research Using JorGPT
screening_decisions.reason: Relevant to automated assessment benchmarking, but the reported dataset is authentic student answers rather than synthetic student responses.

screening_decisions.title: Evaluating LLMs for Automated Scoring in Formative Assessments
screening_decisions.reason: Relevant to LLM automated scoring of real student responses, but not to synthetic student responses.

screening_decisions.title: Leveraging prompt-based LLMs for automated scoring and feedback generation in higher education
screening_decisions.reason: Relevant to prompt-based LLM scoring/feedback, but the targeted pass did not verify synthetic student responses in the scored corpus.

screening_summary: Targeted bootstrap screening included six full-text sources where synthetic, simulated, generated, corrupted, or analogous responses are tied to automated scorer development, training augmentation, pre-pilot validation, label/annotation benchmarking, calibration, or fairness stress testing. Abstract-only and program-only candidates were screened but not used as direct support.
limitations: This was a targeted bootstrap pass, not a systematic review.

Included sources use heterogeneous response settings: constructed responses, EFL essays, short-answer content scoring, adaptive English writing, simulated essays, and an adjacent open-response SJT setting.
Walsh et al. is included as bounded stress-test evidence from admissions SJT scoring rather than ordinary classroom student-response scoring.
Ipci et al. is not used as direct support because this pass verified only repository/abstract-level access.
